title-group.article-title: Prevalence and incidence of post-intensive care syndrome among intensive care unit survivors: a systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: aStudent Research Committee, Department of Medical-Surgical Nursing, School of Nursing and Midwifery, Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran
bDepartment of Medical-Surgical Nursing, School of Nursing and Midwifery, Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

fn.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/07853890.2026.2620880.

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Sogand Sarmadi sogand.sarmadi@ymail.com School of Nursing and Midwifery, Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences, Valiasr Street, intersection with Ayatollah Hashemi Rafsanjani Highway, opposite Shahid Rajaee Heart Hospital, Tehran, Iran

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Post-intensive care syndrome (PICS) is a post-discharge complication from the intensive care unit (ICU) that manifests as a range of physical, cognitive, and psychological impairments for patients. Given the growing number of ICU survivors and the vital role of this syndrome in identifying individuals at risk of deterioration after ICU discharge, we conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis to assess the prevalence and incidence of PICS.

sec.title: Method
sec.p: Between January 1, 2010, and October 5, 2024, a thorough search was conducted across the Web of Science, PubMed, Scopus, Embase, Cochrane Library, and CINAHL databases. Cross-sectional and cohort studies were included. The prevalence and incidence of PICS, as determined by any assessment method, were the primary study outcomes. PICS was defined according to the criteria used in each primary study. A meta-analysis was performed using a random-effects model. Meta-regression analysis was employed to investigate the impact of distinct follow-up durations on the reported prevalence and incidence. The JBI critical appraisal tool for prevalence studies was used to assess the risk of bias in the included studies.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis synthesised data from 34 studies involving 6230 participants. The pooled prevalence and incidence of PICS were 60.3% (95% CI: 48.5–72.1) and 52.4% (95% CI: 47.6–57.2), respectively. The I2 statistic for heterogeneity in the included prevalence and incidence studies was 98.67% and 81.23%, respectively. Subgroup analyses by country, cutoff definition, and underlying participant disease revealed a substantial reduction in heterogeneity. Only the use of a cutoff substantially reduced heterogeneity in reported incidence across studies.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrate a notable prevalence and incidence of PICS among ICU survivors. These findings highlight the need for early detection of at-risk individuals and the development of evidence-based approaches to monitor and address impairments related to PICS.

funding-group.funding-statement: The authors did not receive support from any organization for the submitted work.

sec.title: Introduction:
sec.p: Post-Intensive Care Syndrome (PICS) represents a major and widespread health issue affecting a significant proportion of patients who have been discharged from the intensive care unit (ICU) [1]. Research suggests that a considerable proportion of ICU survivors, ranging from 50% to 80%, experience PICS, which encompasses physical, cognitive, and psychological impairments [2]. These impairments can negatively affect their quality of life (QoL) and ability to engage in daily activities after discharge [1,2].

Physical impairments frequently present as “intensive care unit-acquired weakness” (ICU-AW), encompassing neuromuscular dysfunction [3]. These impairments can compromise essential functions, including swallowing, breathing, and mobility [3,4]. Survivors often experience muscle weakness and fatigue, making it challenging to resume daily activities [1,3]. Many patients (25%-80%) encounter various physical limitations [3]. Psychological symptoms are a significant aspect of PICS, with anxiety, depression, and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) reported in 8%-57% of survivors. Emotional disturbances may arise as early as 24 h after ICU admission and can persist for 5 to 15 years following discharge [4]. Cognitive deficits, including impairments in attention, memory, executive function, and visuospatial perception, may be present in individuals with PICS. Persistent cognitive difficulties can arise as a consequence of delirium, which is frequently encountered during ICU admissions [3].

Accurate diagnosis and assessment of PICS are crucial to facilitate timely interventions and appropriate care for individuals discharged from the ICU [5]. An initial, comprehensive evaluation, conducted by the primary care provider or within specialised PICS clinics, typically initiates the process [4,5]. The primary objective of this assessment is to identify deficits in several key areas, including motor and sensory abilities, communication difficulties, swallowing impairments, post-traumatic stress symptoms, anxiety and depression symptoms, cognitive function, and psychosocial as well as sexual adaptation [5].

Given its long-term effects on patients, their families, and healthcare systems, identifying PICS is paramount [6]. Many factors contribute to the development of PICS [6,7]. Factors contributing to the development of PICS can be categorised into patient-related aspects, including age, gender, pre-existing health conditions, severity of illness, and ICU-related elements [7]. ICU-related factors encompass complications during ICU stay, such as delirium and prolonged mechanical ventilation [8]. Specific conditions encountered during the ICU stay, such as sepsis or shock, may heighten the risk of PICS development [6,8]. A history of mental health issues and advanced age substantially elevate the risk of experiencing PICS symptoms [8]. This emphasises the importance of providing customised follow-up care and recognising the wide variations in the needs of ICU survivors [7,9]. Enhancing the efficiency of managing PICS among ICU survivors requires a more thorough understanding of the risk factors associated with this condition [9].

While PICS is becoming more widely recognised, insufficient awareness among healthcare providers may lead to inadequate follow-up care for survivors [9,10]. Proactive screening and comprehensive management strategies are crucial to achieve improved patient outcomes and enhance their post-ICU QoL [10].

Several systematic reviews have investigated specific domains of PICS. Wolters et al. (2013) reported long-term cognitive impairment in ICU survivors, with prevalence ranging from 4% to 62% over follow-up periods of 2 to 156 months [11]. Anxiety symptoms affect approximately one-third of ICU survivors and persist throughout the first year post-discharge [12]. Depressive symptoms are similarly common, occurring in about 30% of survivors and remaining present up to 12 months after discharge [13]. PTSD symptoms have been reported in 17% to 44% of ICU survivors, with higher prevalence among those with comorbid psychopathology, benzodiazepine exposure, or distressing ICU memories [14].

While these reviews provide essential insights, most focus on individual PICS domains or specific populations, and none have comprehensively synthesised prevalence and incidence across all three core domains and systematically examined sources of heterogeneity. This highlights a critical knowledge gap, which the current systematic review and meta-analysis aims to address by integrating evidence from physical, cognitive, and psychological impairments among ICU survivors worldwide.

The widespread impact of PICS underscores the imperative to resolve it, particularly given the potential for improved recovery trajectories through timely interventions and individualised support tailored to ICU survivors. The emergence of this syndrome and the need to mitigate potential complications necessitate periodic review of research findings to determine the prevalence and incidence of this outcome, thereby providing policymakers with a comprehensive understanding of the current situation. Therefore, a systematic review and meta-analysis were conducted to determine the prevalence and incidence of PICS worldwide.

sec.title: Method
sec.p: This systematic review was reported following the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis) guideline (Table S1) [15]. The protocol was registered with the registration code CRD42024614705 on PROSPERO.

sec.title: Review question
sec.p: The POLIS (Population, Outcome, Location, Indicator, and Study Design) approach addressed the primary review question [16]. The formulated review question was “What is the prevalence and incidence measure of PICS among critically ill patients globally?” The primary aim of this study was to evaluate the prevalence and incidence of PICS among patients after ICU discharge. The secondary objectives of this study included assessing the variability of estimates and identifying potential sources of heterogeneity.

sec.title: Indices measures
sec.p: Prevalence and cumulative incidence were among the indices measured in the included studies.

sec.title: Population
sec.p: Studies were eligible if they reported data on adult ICU survivors assessed after ICU discharge. We included studies in which participants were observed in general wards, attended post-ICU clinics, or were assessed at home after ICU discharge. Studies that enrolled only pediatric participants were excluded. No restrictions were applied regarding ICU length of stay, underlying diagnosis, or follow-up duration. When a study reported multiple follow-up time points, we selected the longest reported follow-up for the primary analyses.

sec.title: Outcome
sec.p: PICS was our primary outcome. Given the multi-component nature of PICS, no restrictions were imposed on the instruments used to assess each domain. Additionally, no restrictions were imposed on the various cutoffs used to diagnose PICS; the unrestricted study selection was intended to minimize publication bias. Given that the primary objective of this study was to ascertain the prevalence and incidence of PICS, any study that failed to report the overall occurrence of PICS was excluded from consideration. Additionally, the definition used to calculate PICS was the presence of an impairment in any PICS domain. Any study lacking the necessary data for this calculation was excluded. Regarding the duration of follow-up, due to the wide variation in follow-up durations, no restriction was applied in the overall meta-analysis; however, the effect of different follow-up durations was evaluated in both meta-regression and subgroup analysis.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p: The review encompassed all descriptive and analytical cross-sectional studies conducted at the population or institutional level. Data derived from the population- or institution-based cohort studies were also eligible. Moreover, secondary analyses based on interventional trials or real-world datasets were considered. In contrast, interventional studies, case–control designs, case reports, case series, and review papers were excluded.

sec.title: Data sources and search strategy
sec.p: Relevant studies were identified through six databases: Web of Science, PubMed, Scopus, Embase, Cochrane Library, and CINAHL. The literature search covered studies published from 1 January 2010 (the first time PICS was introduced [17]) to 5 October 2024, using a combination of MeSH and Emtree terms, the free-text method, and experts’ opinions. Two components, indices and outcomes, were used to formulate the search syntaxes: “post-intensive care syndrome,” “Incidence,” “Incidence Proportion,” “Cumulative Incidence,” and “Prevalence.” The syntaxes were designed using the AND/OR operators. The complete search strategy is available in Table S2. In addition, the reference lists of the included studies and relevant review articles were manually examined to identify any further eligible publications. No restriction was imposed on the language of the included studies. For studies published in languages other than English, Google Translate was employed, and a foreign-language expert was consulted to facilitate data extraction. If further assistance was needed, we contacted the corresponding authors for detailed information in English. To ensure comprehensive searches, Google Scholar was used. Google Scholar was searched using key terms consistent with the primary database strategy to identify potentially relevant studies not indexed in traditional databases, including early online publications and grey literature. The first 200 records, ranked by relevance, were screened.

sec.title: Selection of studies
sec.p: The study selection process was conducted using Hub Meta, a systematic review management platform that facilitates the organisation, screening, and data extraction of literature. All records retrieved from the database searches were first imported into Hub Meta, where duplicate citations were automatically removed, and were subsequently verified by a single reviewer. The initial screening of titles and abstracts was carried out independently by two authors (S.S. and A.Z.). Studies deemed potentially relevant underwent full-text assessment, which was conducted separately by the same reviewers. Any discrepancies arising at any stage of the selection process were resolved by a third reviewer (N.S.), who provided the final judgment. In cases where only abstracts were available, three separate attempts were made to obtain the corresponding full-text articles. Furthermore, to minimise data overlap, when multiple publications were derived from the same data source within an identical time frame, only the study with the largest sample size was included. The exclusion of studies at this review stage was thoroughly documented and justified (Table S1).

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: A standardised data extraction form (see Table S3) was developed following guidance from the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews [18]. The form was piloted on three studies and supplemented to capture variation in methods. Two authors independently extracted the data using the preprepared form (S.S. and A.Z.). The following data were extracted: first author’s name, publication year, study design, country, total population, mean/median age, age range, outcome definitions, diagnostic cut-off level, number of incident or prevalent cases, and follow-up period (time period, average follow-up, or person-years). Disagreements were resolved through discussion, and if consensus could not be reached, a third reviewer made the final decision (N.S.).

sec.title: Risk of bias assessments
sec.p: The methodological quality of the included studies was independently assessed by two reviewers (S.S. and A.Z.) using the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) critical appraisal checklist for prevalence studies [19]. Any discrepancies in judgment were addressed through discussion; when agreement was not reached, a third reviewer (N.S.) was consulted to make the final determination. The JBI checklist comprises nine appraisal items, with responses scored 1 for “yes” and 0 for “no” or “unclear.” For each study, the total score was calculated and presented as a percentage. Based on these percentages and in line with JBI guidelines, studies were categorised as having a high risk of bias when 20–50% of items were rated as “yes,” a moderate risk when 50–80% received “yes” scores, and a low risk when 80–100% of items met the criteria.

sec.title: Data synthesis
sec.p: Prevalence was calculated for each study by dividing the number of participants with the specified outcomes (PICS) by the total number of patients discharged from the ICU. The population at risk of PICS was defined according to the criteria used in each primary study. Within each study, incidence estimates were derived by dividing the number of newly diagnosed PICS cases by the total number of participants at risk at baseline or over the defined observation period. In cases where the number of patients diagnosed with PICS was not explicitly reported, the corresponding values were derived from the available percentages or summary measures. For primary studies reporting estimated PICS prevalence below 10% or above 90%, standard errors were calculated independently using the exact binomial method. Studies were excluded if essential methodological details were missing and attempts to obtain clarification from the authors were unsuccessful, indicating insufficient reporting quality (Table S4). Meta-regression was used to assess the effect of varying follow-up times on the study’s effect measures. The logit incidence or prevalence scale was utilised for meta-regression.

The degree of heterogeneity among studies was assessed using the I2 statistic, which quantifies the proportion of variation in estimates and is applicable only when more than one study is included. A higher I2 value indicates greater heterogeneity between individual study results. We used Cochrane’s framework for interpretation in our study. In particular, heterogeneity was interpreted as low when ranging from 0% to 40%, moderate between 30% and 60%, substantial from 50% to 90%, and considerable when values fell between 75% and 100% [18]. A forest plot was used to present the combined prevalence estimates for PICS, along with their 95% confidence interval (CI).

To investigate potential sources of heterogeneity, we assessed differences among studies across several factors: (i) demographic characteristics such as geographical location; (ii) how the outcomes were defined and measured; (iii) the risk of bias in each study, categorized as low, moderate, or high; (iv) the underlying disease; and (v) COVID-19 status. Data analysis was performed using Stata version 17.

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p: Publication bias was examined using multiple approaches, including visual inspection of funnel plots, Begg’s and Egger’s statistical tests, and the trim-and-fill procedure. Cumulative incidence and prevalence estimates were analysed on the logit scale. Funnel plot analyses were not performed when fewer than ten studies were available. Evidence of publication bias was considered present if either Begg’s or Egger’s test yielded a statistically significant result, with the threshold for significance set at 0.05.

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis
sec.p: Two approaches were employed for sensitivity analyses in this systematic review and meta-analysis. The first involved a leave-one-out method, in which each study was sequentially excluded to evaluate its effect on the overall estimates. The second approach involved conducting separate meta-analyses for studies classified as high- or low-risk of bias.

sec.title: Selection process
sec.p: An initial database search yielded 5,863 studies. Hub Meta facilitated the removal of 3,888 duplicate records. Seven hundred thirty-seven studies were excluded during the title and abstract screening phase for being irrelevant to the research focus. Additionally, 1,146 studies were excluded due to inappropriate study designs. Of the 92 studies chosen for full-text retrieval, two articles lacked accessible full text. Then, 57 articles were excluded for inappropriate study design or intervention, or for possible overlap in the data analysed (after reading the full text) (Table S5). Additionally, one study was retrieved through hand searching. The review ultimately encompassed 34 studies (Figure 1) [7,20–52].

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis comprised 34 studies, 26 of which focused on prevalence and eight on incidence. The prevalence studies included 2,668 participants, while the incidence studies included 3,562 participants [7,20–52]. This review encompasses studies published in various countries between 2016 and 2024. Of the thirty-four studies, four were published before 2020, with the remaining thirty published after 2020. Twelve studies were performed in Asia, including seven in Japan [21,25,27,29,40,47,53], two in Korea [7,26], and one in India, Turkey, and China. Thirteen studies were conducted in Europe: three in Switzerland [23,45,51] and four in Spain [35,39,41,44]. The remaining research was conducted in Germany, Italy, France, the Netherlands, and Portugal. A total of six studies were undertaken in North America, with five in the USA [22,31–33,46] and one in Canada. The remaining research was performed in Australia and South America. The study’s sample sizes varied from 13 to 2,345 participants. The definition of PICS in most studies involved a deterioration in at least one physical, cognitive, or psychological domain. The distribution of follow-up intervals across the included studies is presented in Table S6. Among prevalence studies (n = 26), early follow-up (0–2.99 months post-ICU discharge) was reported in five studies (19.2%), 3–5.99 months in three studies (11.5%), 6–11.99 months in eight studies (30.8%), 12–23.99 months in six studies (23.1%), and ≥24 months in three studies (11.5%). Among incidence studies (n = 8), early follow-up was reported in one study (12.5%), 12–23.99 months in four studies (50.0%), and ≥24 months in three studies (37.5%), with no studies reporting follow-up at 3–5.99 or 6–11.99 months. Table 1 and Table S6 present a comprehensive overview of the key features and findings of the included studies.

caption.p: Summary characteristics of studies investigating PICS incidence and prevalence in ICU survivors.

sec.title: Risk of bias evaluation of the included studies
sec.p: The JBI tool for prevalence studies was applied to evaluate the quality of the studies. A risk of bias was clear in the sampling method of all studies. Twelve studies demonstrated no evidence of bias stemming from inappropriate sampling methods. All studies, excluding four, presented a possible bias in their sample sizes. The studies were free of potential bias resulting from participant selection or study setting, except for Argento et al.’s research [20]. None of the studies demonstrated potential bias from insufficient coverage of the identified sample during analysis—all studies, except for Argento et al. [20], employed methods for condition identification that did not exhibit potential bias. Three studies have identified potential biases arising from the reliability of condition measurement [34,36,39]. Bias was identified in the statistical analysis across twelve research studies [21,22,24,26,29–31,33,36,40,42]. A potential bias due to inadequate response rates was identified in only three studies [20,24,49]. The JBI analysis identified 28 studies with moderate risk, 3 with high risk, and 3 with low risk (Table 2).

caption.p: Risk of bias in included studies.

fn.p: Risk of Bias Legend: 1. Bias arising from the sample frame, 2. Bias arising from inappropriate sampling, 3. Bias from a sample size, 4. Bias due to study subjects and setting, 5. Bias due to analysis conducted with insufficient coverage of the identified sample, 6. Bias due to methods used for the identification of the condition, 7. Bias in the reliability of the measurement of the condition, 8.Bias during statistical analysis, 9. Bias arising from response rate adequacy; Y: Yes, N: No, U: Unclear.

sec.title: Pooled prevalence and incidence of post-intensive care syndrome‌
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis found a pooled prevalence of PICS of 60.3% (95% CI: 48.5–72.1). The I2 statistic, used to estimate heterogeneity across studies, yielded a value of 98.67%. The Cochrane Q test revealed significant heterogeneity among studies concerning prevalence estimation (Figure 2).

caption.p: Forest plot for pooled prevalence of PICS-Total.

sec.p: The incidence of PICS was 52.4% (95% CI: 47.6–57.2). The I2 statistic, used to quantify heterogeneity among studies, was 81.23%. Significant heterogeneity in estimates of PICS incidence was demonstrated by the Cochrane Q test (p < 0.001) (Figure 3).

caption.p: Forest plot for pooled incidence of PICS-Total.

sec.title: Geographical-related factors
sec.p: Continental subgroup analysis revealed a PICS prevalence of 48.7% in Asia, 64.7% in Europe, and 76.3% in North America. A subgroup analysis of the continental subgroup showed a modest reduction in heterogeneity (I2 = 91.46%, p < 0.001). A country-specific analysis indicated that the prevalence of PICS was 55.9% in Japan, 74.4% in the USA, and 51.4% in Switzerland. A subgroup analysis by country demonstrated a significant reduction in heterogeneity within that subgroup (I2 = 65.28%, p < 0.001). A country-specific subgroup analysis could plausibly account for the notable heterogeneity in the overall prevalence estimates.

sec.title: Clinical factors
sec.p: Analysis of participant data, stratified by underlying disease, revealed a PICS prevalence of 72.7% in COVID-19 patients, 48.7% in Cardiovascular patients, 97.4% in sepsis survivors, and 45.4% in general ICU patients. Subgroup analysis by participant characteristics indicated a significant reduction in heterogeneity within the defined subgroup (I2 = 87.32%, p < 0.001). An analysis of subgroups stratified by patients’ underlying diseases could illuminate the considerable variability in overall prevalence.

sec.title: Methodology-related factors
sec.p: A subgroup analysis of the data collection methods revealed that the prevalence of PICS was 66.9% via telephone, 42.0% via face-to-face contact, 63.0% via medical records, and 75.2% via postal questionnaire. Subgroup analysis, categorised by data collection method, indicated reduced heterogeneity among studies using the face-to-face method (I2 = 91.27%, p = 0.001). Data-collection-method-based subgroup analysis could elucidate the significant heterogeneity observed in the overall prevalence.

Subgroup analysis using a distinct PICS definition revealed a prevalence of 60.2% in studies employing a specific definition and 60.5% in studies employing an ambiguous meaning. Subgroup analysis based on a particular PICS definition did not reduce heterogeneity for the targeted subgroup.

Examining subgroups stratified by study design indicated a PICS prevalence of 63.4% in prospective and 56.8% in retrospective investigations. Analysis of subgroups defined by the study design did not reveal diminished heterogeneity. Analysis of subgroups defined by PICS did not account for the substantial heterogeneity in overall prevalence. The presence or absence of defined instrument cut-offs revealed a PICS prevalence of 67.0% in studies employing a cut-off and 37.6% in studies without a cut-off. Further analysis of subgroups defined by instrument cutoff points did not reveal reduced heterogeneity within any subgroup.

A subgroup analysis based on the COVID-19 timeline indicated PICS prevalence of 63.4% before the pandemic, 69.1% after, and 52.9% across both periods. In addition, subgroup analysis by COVID-19 time showed a slight reduction in heterogeneity among post COVID-19 studies (I2 = 92.36%, p < 0.001).

A subgroup analysis based on the risk-of-bias assessment using the JBI tool indicated that the prevalence of PICS was 46.7% in studies classified as high risk, 59.2% in studies classified as moderate risk, and 97.2% in studies classified as low risk. The application of subgroup analysis, using JBI’s risk-of-bias reporting, failed to demonstrate a reduction in heterogeneity within a particular subgroup. Detailed information on the subgroup analysis of prevalence studies is provided in Table 3.

caption.p: Sub-Group results of factors contributing to variations in estimated prevalence among included studies.

tr.th: No. of included studies in each sub-category

sec.p: Analyses based on precise follow-up durations demonstrated that standardising follow-up durations marginally reduced heterogeneity; however, this reduction was not statistically significant. Additionally, the prevalence increased with longer follow-up periods.

Regarding follow-up windows, the pooled prevalence of PICS varied depending on the timing of follow-up assessments. Studies with very early follow-up (up to three months) reported a prevalence of approximately 78%, while those assessed between three and six months exhibited a similar prevalence. Notably, studies with follow-up periods of six to twenty-four months reported a prevalence of approximately 48%, indicating some improvement over time. Conversely, studies with follow-up beyond twenty-four months demonstrated a rebound in prevalence to approximately 69%, suggesting that specific impairments may persist or re-emerge in the long term. It is also noteworthy that heterogeneity among studies was reduced within these follow-up subgroups, implying that the timing of assessment accounts for some of the variability in reported prevalence.

sec.title: Incidence subgroup analyses
sec.p: Although analyses were conducted for eight subgroups, I2 reduction was observed in three. Categorizing studies by time of conduction (pre-COVID or post-COVID), presence or absence of a specific cutoff for employed tools, and follow-up duration largely explained the heterogeneity among included studies. It was also demonstrated that, in a subgroup analysis by follow-up duration, I2 fell below 60%, indicating very substantial heterogeneity. Also, studies with longer follow-up reported a higher incidence of PICS. A detailed analysis of the subgroups is presented in Table 4.

caption.p: Sub-Group results of factors contributing to variations in estimated incidence among included studies.

tr.th: No. of included studies in each sub-category

sec.title: Meta-regression analysis
sec.p: Meta-regression analysis did not reveal a statistically significant correlation between follow-up duration and reported prevalence (p = 0.961). In addition, meta-regression analysis incorporating follow-up duration (months) did not significantly reduce heterogeneity among studies (residual I2 = 96.79%). Follow-up duration showed no predictive ability for the calculated prevalence (R2 = 0.00) (Figure S1).

Furthermore, the reported incidence increased with longer follow-up duration (weeks), although this association was not statistically significant (p = 0.190). The heterogeneity across studies remained substantial despite incorporating follow-up duration in the meta-regression model. Furthermore, meta-regression analyses indicated that follow-up duration was not a significant predictor of incidence, accounting for 1.98% of the variance. Detailed information on meta-regression is provided in the Supplementary Appendix.

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p: Publication bias and an asymmetrical distribution of studies used to estimate prevalence were evident in the funnel plot (Figure S2). As the number of incidence studies was insufficient, funnel plots were deemed inappropriate for evaluation. The Begg and Egger tests showed statistically significant results (p = 0.0063 and p = 0.000, respectively) in the prevalence studies, suggesting a potential risk of publication bias. A trim-and-fill test was employed. Trim-and-fill analysis for prevalence did not identify additional studies, indicating a low risk of publication bias. While Begg’s test did not yield a statistically significant result (p = 0.901), Egger’s test was statistically significant (p = 0.0243), suggesting potential publication bias in the incidence estimates (Supplementary Appendix).

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis
sec.p: A leave-one-out sensitivity analysis was conducted. The exclusion of the Koselik et al. and Weidman et al. study [28,46] decreased overall prevalence, reaching 59% (Figure S3). Also, eliminating Venni et al. and Flaws et al. [24,43] increased overall prevalence to 62%. Additionally, an alternative strategy was employed by excluding studies at high risk of bias. This didn’t result in a significant difference in pooled prevalence (Figure S4). Using a leave-one-out remove method for incidence estimates showed that omitting studies by Torres et al. [42] and Gimenez-Esparza Vich et al. [44] resulted in a significant alteration in incidence estimates (Figure S5).

sec.title: Domain specific prevalence
sec.p: The prevalence of PICS varies among its primary domains—namely, cognitive, psychological, and physical impairments. Aggregated data suggest that approximately one-third of ICU survivors develop cognitive impairments, with a prevalence of 32.7% (95% CI: 23.9–41.5%) derived from 19 studies. Psychological impairments, including affect, were reported in approximately 28.0% of survivors (95% CI: 21.5–34.6%), based on 16 studies. Physical impairments are somewhat more prevalent, with a pooled prevalence of 38.4% (95% CI: 28.5–48.3%) across 16 studies.

Significant variability was observed across studies within all three domains. Estimates of cognitive impairment ranged from 2.7% to 62.1%, mental health impairments from 5.7% to 58.1%, and physical impairments from 11.0% to 86.7%. This heterogeneity reflects differences in patient characteristics, ICU settings, assessment instruments, and follow-up durations. Statistical analyses confirmed considerable between-study variability, with I2 values of 95.5% for cognitive, 90.3% for mental health, and 95.6% for physical domains. The elevated heterogeneity suggests that the observed differences are primarily attributable to genuine variation across studies rather than random chance, emphasizing the necessity for standardized assessment tools and consistent follow-up protocols in future research.

sec.title: Domain specific incidence
sec.p: A meta-analysis of four studies on cognitive impairments in ICU survivors revealed a pooled incidence of 17.2% (95% CI: 3.0–31.5%), with individual estimates ranging from 3.2% to 29.4%. Heterogeneity was markedly high (I2 = 98.0%, p < 0.001), indicating considerable disparities across patient populations, assessment methods, and follow-up durations. Regarding mental health issues, the incidence across the three studies was 23.0% (95% CI: 0.70–46.6%), with significant heterogeneity (I2 = 98.9%, p < 0.001), attributable to variations in measurement instruments, follow-up periods, and study contexts. The incidence of physical impairments was documented in four studies, yielding a pooled estimate of 22.4% (95% CI: 8.6–36.3%), with individual estimates ranging from 4.9% to 34.3%. Heterogeneity remained substantial (I2 = 97.5%, p < 0.001).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrate that PICS is a highly prevalent and persistent condition among survivors of ICU, affecting a substantial proportion of patients after discharge. The findings indicate that impairments across physical, cognitive, and psychological domains commonly coexist and continue beyond the acute phase of critical illness, underscoring PICS as a significant long-term consequence of ICU rather than a transient complication.

Considerable variability was observed across studies, reflecting differences in patient populations, clinical contexts, outcome definitions, and assessment methods. This variability is mainly due to the wide range of instruments used to measure PICS and differing diagnostic cut-offs, which may compromise comparability and reliability across studies. Subgroup analyses suggested that underlying diagnosis, geographical setting, and methodological issues contribute meaningfully to this heterogeneity. Notably, populations exposed to severe systemic illness and pandemic-related ICU care appeared to experience a greater burden of post-ICU impairments, highlighting the influence of contextual and healthcare system factors on recovery trajectories.

Although heterogeneity and potential publication bias limit the precision of pooled estimates, the overall body of evidence consistently indicates that PICS represents a widespread and clinically significant challenge for ICU survivors. It is important to note that most of the included studies were rated as having a moderate risk of bias. This moderate methodological quality introduces a risk of bias that may affect the certainty of the evidence.

The comparison between the overall prevalence of PICS and domain-specific impairments offers valuable insights into the burden and characteristics of this condition. While the pooled prevalence of overall PICS was approximately 60%, analyses of specific domains indicated that physical impairments were the most common (38%), followed by cognitive impairments (33%) and mental health disturbances (28%). This underscores the fact that not all patients diagnosed with PICS exhibit deficits across all domains, thereby emphasizing the heterogeneity of post-ICU sequelae. Overall, the pooled incidence of PICS was lower than the prevalence, at approximately 52%, reflecting that not all ICU survivors develop new impairments after discharge.

Domain-specific incidence estimates were somewhat lower: physical impairments affected 22%, mental health impairments 23%, and cognitive impairments 17% of patients. These findings indicate that new-onset impairments are common but do not necessarily affect all domains simultaneously.

From both research and clinical perspectives, the adoption of core outcome sets, standardised assessment instruments, and clearly defined thresholds is essential. Future studies should explicitly report which domains are assessed, whether PICS is defined as impairment in any versus all domains, and the follow-up intervals used. Such standardisation will improve comparability across studies, enhance the precision of prevalence and incidence estimates, and facilitate the identification of high-risk patients for targeted interventions.

The complexity of PICS, with multiple components, poses a significant hurdle to integrating evidence from different studies. This is primarily due to the wide range of instruments and differing cut-off criteria. This poses a potential threat to the reliability of PICS measurements in diverse research endeavours. Nonetheless, two instruments comprehensively assess the event’s three components: the Healthy Ageing Brain Care Monitor and the Post-Intensive Care Syndrome Questionnaire [54,55]. It is important to note that none of the studies examined in our systematic review utilised these instruments. This indicates a relative lack of attention given to these instruments. A well-defined framework for measuring this syndrome would contribute to the reliability of our findings.

An additional consideration stemming from our findings is the significant lack of standardisation in outcome measurement across studies assessing PICS. This challenge closely mirrors concerns previously articulated regarding survivors of acute respiratory failure. Needham et al. addressed this issue by proposing a core outcome measurement set for post-discharge research, with the objective of reducing heterogeneity and improving comparability among studies through the consistent assessment of key long-term outcomes. Their consensus-based framework emphasises the importance of systematically evaluating mental health, pain, life satisfaction, and health-related QoL using validated instruments [56].

The considerable variability observed in the instruments, thresholds, and domains assessed in the studies included in the present review reflects an unresolved methodological challenge and likely contributed to the heterogeneity of our pooled estimates. Aligning future research on PICS with established core outcome recommendations would enhance methodological rigour, facilitate meaningful evidence synthesis, and support more reliable identification of patients at risk of long-term impairment. Specifically, future studies should utilise validated instruments, adopt consensus-based cut-off scores, and harmonise assessment methods across centres to reduce heterogeneity and improve comparability. Such standardisation will enable more accurate prevalence estimates and more effective evaluation of interventions targeting PICS [56].

In accordance with customary expectations for descriptive meta-analyses, substantial heterogeneity was observed among the studies reviewed. Consequently, the pooled prevalence should be regarded as a general indicator of the overall burden of PICS across varied clinical and methodological contexts, rather than as a precise estimate applicable to any specific population.

Subgroup analyses demonstrated that the presence or absence of a specific cutoff value accounted for a substantial portion of the variability observed between studies. Notably, the observed heterogeneity appears to be mainly systematic and clinically meaningful, reflecting fundamental differences between study populations and assessment approaches rather than random error. Studies that omitted a precise threshold tended to underreport prevalence and thereby increase heterogeneity. Geographic subgroup analyses indicated that prevalence estimates were more homogeneous in Asian countries, particularly Japan. Despite these subgroup analyses, some residual heterogeneity persisted. Although these analyses did not eliminate heterogeneity, they provide essential context for interpreting the pooled estimates and support the robustness of the overall conclusions. It is noteworthy that approximately 42% of the studies included involved patients who received treatment during the initial waves of the COVID-19 pandemic. Patients admitted to ICUs during this epoch encountered exceptional clinical and social circumstances—including markedly high disease severity, altered sedation and ventilation protocols, restricted family visitation, and widespread psychosocial stressors—which may have impacted the nature and frequency of post-ICU sequelae, primarily affecting cognitive and mental health outcomes. Therefore, pooled prevalence estimates should be approached with caution, and contextual factors pertinent to the pandemic period should be taken into account when extrapolating these findings.

Our findings are consistent with prior evidence regarding post-ICU sequelae. Long-term cognitive impairment following ICU admission has been reported in 4–62% of patients in the systematic review by Wolters et al. (2013), with variability attributable primarily to differences in assessment tools and follow-up duration, findings that align with the heterogeneity observed in our study [11]. Similarly, the prevalence of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD), anxiety, and depressive symptoms in ICU survivors has been reported as approximately 17–44%, 32–40%, and 29–34%, respectively [12–14], supporting the high burden of PICS observed in our review.

Additionally, our research revealed a significantly higher prevalence of PICS among patients who had recovered from sepsis and COVID-19. A systematic review of risk factors for PICS development identified a strong association between disease severity and the incidence of PICS-related physical impairments. These results align with those obtained in our study [10]. Additional studies have conclusively demonstrated the profound impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the well-being of ICU-discharged individuals and their relatives [57]. PICS has been viewed as a crisis emerging after a crisis [58]. These findings align with our study results, which demonstrated a higher prevalence of PICS among individuals who had recovered from COVID-19. Additional subgroup analyses failed to elucidate the substantial heterogeneity observed. Future research could explore a more comprehensive set of factors to mitigate heterogeneity.

The growing population of ICU survivors suggests that the phenomenon under discussion will become increasingly prevalent. Therefore, future studies should focus on developing solutions to mitigate the incidence and prevalence of PICS-related impairments. Our research, alongside similar studies, aims to elucidate the scope of the problem. Moreover, studies on risk factors enhance our capacity to identify individuals at risk, implement screening, and provide further interventions to reduce impairments. Research has also investigated the efficacy of non-pharmacological interventions, including early mobilisation and physical rehabilitation in the ICU, ICU diaries, psychological interventions, post-ICU multidisciplinary clinics and interventions, and educational activities in reducing impairments [5,59].

A systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to evaluate the effectiveness of ICU-initiated transitional care interventions for patients and families on PICS elements. The findings revealed a significant lack of research examining the effectiveness of transitional care interventions specifically for ICU patients experiencing PICS [60]. A second review concluded that interventions demonstrating substantial efficacy in patients following ICU are infrequent. They stated that ICU-diary interventions showed positive outcomes for patients experiencing PTSD. Nevertheless, more interventional studies involving a larger cohort of ICU survivors are required [61]. A contributing factor to inadequate recovery following discharge from the ICU may be the delayed provision of care and treatment. E-health has the potential to enhance access to care and facilitate continuous remote support. A systematic review and meta-analysis revealed that e-health interventions show promise for facilitating rehabilitation in the early stages after critical care, with preliminary evidence supporting their feasibility [62].

Given the high prevalence and incidence of the outcome examined in our study, we urge conducting future intervention studies to assess the effectiveness of different interventions with greater confidence. Implementation of these interventions is expected to reduce the prevalence and incidence of such phenomena.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: Several limitations warrant emphasis. First, there is no universally accepted operational definition of PICS; studies included in our review used varying domain definitions and thresholds, which complicates pooling and interpretation of prevalence and incidence estimates. Second, the instruments used to assess each PICS domain varied widely in scope and psychometric properties. Combining results from such heterogeneous instruments into a single pooled estimate risks oversimplification and may produce biased or non-comparable prevalence estimates. Third, it should be emphasised that the relatively small number of studies reporting domain-specific estimates, along with variability in PICS definitions, limited our ability to perform consistent “any versus all domains” recalculations across studies. Consequently, certainty for some pooled estimates is limited. Fourth, no included studies were conducted in African settings, limiting geographic generalizability. Fifth, when individual studies reported multiple follow-up time points, we selected the longest reported follow-up for the primary analyses; this decision may preferentially capture later outcomes, thereby overestimating prevalence and incidence or obscuring significant temporal trends, and it may contribute to between-study heterogeneity. Finally, our publication-bias assessments suggested a small-study effect, and the JBI risk-of-bias appraisal identified methodological limitations in several primary studies; both issues reduce confidence in the pooled estimates.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Our study indicated a prevalence of PICS of 60.3%. The incidence of PICS was also 52.4%. Most included studies were of moderate risk of bias, which may reduce the certainty of these estimates. A statistically significantly higher prevalence was observed in individuals who had recovered from COVID-19, sepsis, and neurological ICU compared to other populations. Prevalence studies demonstrated considerable heterogeneity. The heterogeneity observed may be attributable to subgroup analyses conducted by continent, country, underlying disease, and instrument cutoff definition. Meta-regression analyses examining the influence of follow-up duration yielded no statistically significant findings. The results of our study indicated the possibility of a small study effect. Considering the high prevalence of PICS in ICU survivors and the limited number of studies evaluating intervention effectiveness, future research should prioritize high-quality interventional studies. It is imperative to determine whether these interventions should be integrated into standard care, based on the strength of the evidence.

sec.title: Consent to publish
sec.p: Not Applicable.

sec.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
sec.p: Not Applicable.

sec.title: Permission to reproduce material from other sources
sec.p: There are no materials from other sources.

sec.title: Registration
sec.p: The protocol is registered in the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO) (Registration Code = CRD42024614705).

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: The datasets generated and analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
